title: Transactions
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Transactions Transactions play a particular role for stateful components, because these components usually remain active for long periods of time while passing through various states. In principle, each state requires that resources be allocated to it. Logical transactions From a business point of view, logical transactions are defined using activity diagrams and state diagrams. A logical transaction typically relates to a sequence of activities in the activity diagram. A logical transaction starts when a set of data is accessed that is consistent from a business point of view. During the transaction, the data set is usually not consistent because of the modifications that take place. The logical transaction ends at the next consistent state. However, data consistency can only be enforced by correctly implementing the logical transaction as a technical transaction. To roll back work to the previous consistent state, the logical transaction must relate to a single technical transaction. Technical transactions Technical transactions implement logical transactions and are defined in terms of the implementation products. A meaningful definition of a technical transaction requires the definition of the associated logical transaction, because the technical transaction must agree with the business requirements and business rules. A technical transaction starts when the first data locks are acquired and finishes when the locks are released. The precise moments of acquiring and releasing locks depend on how the transaction is modeled and implemented, the implementation tool used, and the DBMS. To keep the transaction size to a minimum, a technical transaction should implement a single logical transaction. Thus, logical and technical transactions have a one-to-one relationship. Example A simple example of a logical transaction is the process of adding orderlines to an order. The class diagram defines a business rule that an orderline must have an associated order. This business rule applies to the logical transaction. As a consequence, the technical transaction must define a lock that prohibits the removal of an order for which order lines are being entered, and it must execute a commit or rollback instruction after all order lines have been entered.
